message.content: when i'm checking out someone elses branch for review, what is the process? git checkout branch-name, and then should I pull? or does thecheckout bring the new fiels and whatnot onto my computer?

content.text:
When you checkout someone else's branch, you need to fetch it first, then checkout. Here's the process:

```bash
git fetch origin
git checkout branch-name
```

The `git fetch` downloads the branch from the remote but doesn't merge anything. The `git checkout` then switches to that branch locally.

Alternatively, you can do it in one command:
```bash
git checkout -b branch-name origin/branch-name
```

Or if the branch already exists locally but might be outdated:
```bash
git checkout branch-name
git pull origin branch-name
```

The checkout alone doesn't download new files - it only switches to a branch that already exists in your local repository. You need `fetch` or `pull` to get updates from the remote.

message.content:
i guess i was looking at my coworkers stuff and we never use fetch

i just used git checkout coworkers-branch-name

and then that was a little outdated so I did git pull and i got what i needed

content.text:
You're right! If the branch already exists on the remote, `git checkout branch-name` will automatically create a local tracking branch and switch to it. This works because Git knows to look for `origin/branch-name` when you don't have a local branch with that name.

Then `git pull` updates it with the latest changes from the remote - exactly what you did.

This is the simpler workflow most teams use:
```bash
git checkout branch-name
git pull
```

The `fetch` approach I mentioned is more explicit but not necessary for most day-to-day work.

message.content:
ok cool, but then when I switch back to master, should i do...

git checkout master
git pull

in order to reset the world to the current state of things?

content.text:
Yes, exactly! 

```bash
git checkout master
git pull
```

This switches you back to master and updates it with the latest changes from the remote. That way you're back to the current state of the main branch.

This is the standard workflow when switching between branches - always pull after checkout to make sure you have the latest changes for that branch.
